title: Willow log basket
subtitle: A large stake-and-strand willow basket for firewood
excerpt: A round willow log basket: 40 cm diameter base, 30 cm sides, handle arches at each end. This is the standard intermediate willow project; large enough to carry split logs, sturdy enough to sit by the fire without a stand. The method is stake-and-strand with a pairing weave for the sides and a four-rod waling band at the border.
sourceNotes: Traditional British stake-and-strand willow basketwork; Cassell's Household Guide (1880) vol. II for log basket proportions and border construction.

content.text: A log basket tests whether you have the pairing weave and the round base in hand from the technique guide. The weight of a full load of logs shows up any weak base or stake at once. The build uses brown or buff willow for the sturdiest result. Green willow works too, but the basket will tighten as it dries. The handle arches are a traditional touch. They make the basket easy to carry without a stand.
The willow

Brown willow (dried, unsteamed) is the standard material for a log basket: strong enough for the load, rough-barked enough for grip. Soak brown willow for 2 hours before use and leave under a damp cloth for 30 minutes after soaking to allow the moisture to equalise through the rod. Buff willow (steamed, smoother surface) needs only 30 minutes soaking. The rod sizes for a 40 cm base log basket: base sticks 8 mm diameter, 60 cm long (10 sticks); side stakes 6 to 7 mm diameter, 90 cm long (20 stakes); weavers 4 to 5 mm diameter, 90 to 120 cm long (40 rods); handle rods 10 to 12 mm diameter, 100 cm long (2 rods).
Tools
A bodkin for opening the slath holes, inserting stakes, and threading the handle ends. A sloyd knife for trimming rod ends flush. A leather strop. No other tools are required.
Method

Build in four stages: base, staking up, sides, border. Complete the base before staking up; complete the sides before the border. Rushing between stages produces a basket that is not square at any level.
Build the round base using the slath technique (10 base sticks, 5 and 5 through each other) worked to 40 cm diameter with the pairing weave. The base must lie flat before staking up begins.
Stake up: use the bodkin to open a hole alongside each base stick end at the base edge. Insert a 90 cm side
stake

into each hole, driving it 8 cm into the base weave. Bend all stakes upward and tie their tips together temporarily with string to hold them upright. There should be 20 stakes evenly distributed around the base perimeter.
Work a 4-row
waling

band at the base: this locks the stakes in position and establishes the upward angle of the sides. Three waling rods are inserted at 3 adjacent stakes and worked around the full basket, each rod going over 2 stakes and behind 1. Complete 4 rows of waling.
Work the sides with the
pairing

weave until the sides reach 30 cm. Maintain even outward flare; the sides should angle out at roughly 10 degrees from vertical, not grow straight up. Check the flare angle every 5 rows by measuring the rim diameter against the base diameter.
Work a 2-row waling band at the top to consolidate the rim before the border. The waling band prevents the pairing rows from spreading when the border is worked.

Work the border: fold each stake down to the right and thread it behind the next 3 stakes, then bring it through to the inside. Work around the full basket. Trim all stake ends close on the inside with the sloyd knife.

Insert the handle arches: use the bodkin to open a tunnel through the border on opposite sides of the basket. Thread a thick rod (10 to 12 mm, 100 cm long) through the tunnel on each side, arch it across the opening, and secure the other end through the opposite border tunnel. Wrap each arch with a fine weaver, working in an even spiral from one end to the other.
Finishing

Leave the finished basket to dry completely in a warm room for 3 days. The willow will lighten in colour as it dries and the weave will tighten slightly. Do not use until fully dry; a damp basket left in use will compress at the base under load.
Variations
Shopping basket: same construction, 25 cm diameter base, 20 cm sides. Single handle arch only.
Buff willow: paler colour, smoother surface. Otherwise identical construction; reduce soaking time to 30 minutes.
Care

Spray lightly with water if the basket becomes very dry and brittle in a centrally heated room; a brief misting re-flexes the willow without soaking it. Do not immerse in water. The basket will last for many years if kept dry between uses.

attrs.intro: Common problems with the willow log basket.

items.fix: A domed base cannot be corrected after completion. In future, maintain firm pairing tension from the first round and check the base lies flat before staking up.
items.cause: Pairing tension too loose in the base, or base too thin for the basket diameter
items.symptom: Base domes upward in the centre when the basket is loaded

items.fix: Work the next pairing round at a deliberately wider angle and check the flare against the base diameter every 5 rows. Soak the basket briefly to soften the stakes if they will not flex outward.
items.cause: Waling band at the base too tight, or stakes bent at too sharp an angle during staking up
items.symptom: Stakes lean inward rather than outward as the sides are worked

items.fix: Work the 2-row waling band carefully before the border to consolidate the top of the sides. Loose stakes in the border indicate the final pairing rows were too loose.
items.cause: Pairing weave not at consistent tension at the top of the sides
items.symptom: Border works unevenly; some stakes are loose, others tight

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
